interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist (Southwest Georgia Project / Albany Movement)
interview_metadata.interview_location: Albany, Georgia (campus of Albany State University)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Ohio State University and the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Robert McClary, interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries on March 9, 2013 in Albany, Georgia for the Civil Rights History Project (a joint undertaking of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress), gives a brief account of his childhood and his involvement with the civil rights movement in Southwest Georgia under organizer Charles Sherrod. McClary recalls being born at Phoebe Putney Hospital and the early loss of his mother, after which his father, who held a job on the river, relocated the surviving family. He recalls his father's own confrontation with a white man, which McClary references as a formative memory of family resistance to racial intimidation.

McClary describes joining the movement in the early 1960s when he became aware of organized civil rights activity in Albany, including an incident involving a young Black woman and a young white man that he frames as part of the early organizing he saw locally. He recounts that the local organizing was identified as the Albany Movement and that he was drawn into the work, eventually joining Charles Sherrod and what he describes as Sherrod's effort to organize roughly nine counties in Southwest Georgia, with a goal of taking over one of the counties through local electoral organizing. McClary describes accompanying organizers to help residents who were being denied welfare, doing welfare appeals work, and working with tenants threatened with eviction. He also recalls an attempted desegregation action at a local cafe he calls a casino, where Black customers were routed to a back window rather than served at the counter.

McClary recounts being physically attacked while sleeping at the movement office, when a man broke in, beat him while interrogating him about another organizer's whereabouts, and was later taken into custody by police the same night after another staff member intervened. Asked at the end whether he had been afraid, McClary says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid, and he frames his father — whom he describes as having physically confronted a white man and remained in town afterward — as the model for that lack of fear.

This interview transcript is severely degraded by automatic-speech-recognition errors (estimated 60-70 percent of segments are incoherent or garbled), with extensive repetition artifacts (e.g., many segments collapsed to the placeholder 'A...' or to repeated phrases such as 'I was born on October 19th'), and most place names, organizational names, and proper nouns beyond Charles Sherrod, the Albany Movement, Phoebe Putney Hospital, and Albany State University cannot be confidently reconstructed from the SRT text alone. This summary covers only what could be reconstructed with reasonable confidence; substantive portions of McClary's testimony are not recoverable from this transcript and would require re-transcription from source audio before publication.
key_themes: Southwest Georgia Movement and Charles Sherrod's organizing
Racial violence and intimidation against organizers
Family modeling of resistance to racial subordination
Hope and excitement as motivating affect in movement work
main_summary.historical_significance: Robert McClary appears to have been a grassroots participant in the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project led by Charles Sherrod, the canonical 1961-1965 organizing effort that grew out of the Albany Movement. If recoverable through re-transcription, his testimony would contribute documentation of welfare-rights and tenant-advocacy work in Southwest Georgia counties beyond the well-documented Albany city campaigns, an understudied dimension of the SW Georgia organizing record. The current Whisper-degraded transcript does not support publication-grade extraction of his contribution; routing to human review and re-transcription is the appropriate disposition.

errors: Several proper nouns in the transcript are Whisper-degraded and cannot be confidently resolved (e.g., the 'casino' cafe name, the specific Southwest Georgia counties Sherrod organized, the names of co-organizers besides Sherrod, the place 'Mother's Beach' which may be a Whisper error for an actual street/address).

The transcript twice reads 'Albany Movement, Georgia' and 'Albany Movement State University' where the context unambiguously requires 'Albany, Georgia' and 'Albany State University' — the Whisper system appears to have substituted 'Movement' for the city name in some segments.

Many narrative claims (e.g., McClary's age, the exact county targeted for the local takeover, the identity of the New York activist who came down, the name of the person who attacked him at the office) cannot be reconstructed from the transcript and are deliberately omitted from this summary.
improvements: Re-transcribe from source audio with a higher-accuracy ASR model or human transcription before any further summarization. Pass 7 OPEN_PROBLEMS Problem 1a flags this transcript as severely degraded.

After re-transcription, verify the SW Georgia county targeted, the casino/cafe name, the name of the attacker at the office, and any other named organizers, against Charles Sherrod's published memoir and SNCC organizational records.
Confirm whether McClary worked specifically with Shirley Miller Sherrod's Baker County organizing (1965-1966) or with the earlier Albany Movement core, both of which Charles Sherrod led.

chapters.title: Library of Congress Interview Framing
chapters.summary: The opening segments identify the recording as a Civil Rights History Project interview, a joint undertaking of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress. Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries of Ohio State University and the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill introduces himself and videographer Petna Dalico, names the date as Saturday, March 9, 2013, and identifies the location as the campus of Albany State University in Albany, Georgia. He introduces Mr. Robert McClary as the interviewee.
related_events: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture
Southern Oral History Program (UNC Chapel Hill)

errors: Transcript reads 'Albany Movement, Georgia' and 'Albany Movement State University' where Whisper has substituted 'Movement' for the city name; the canonical names are 'Albany, Georgia' and 'Albany State University.' The summary uses the canonical forms.
improvements: Confirm spelling of videographer name 'Petna Dalico' from independent source.

chapters.title: Birth, Childhood, and Loss of Mother
chapters.summary: McClary describes being born on October 19 at Phoebe Putney Hospital. He recalls that his mother died when he was very young (the transcript references her death but the surrounding context is severely garbled). His father held a job on the river and moved what remained of the family afterward. The chapter is heavily Whisper-degraded with multiple repetition artifacts ('I was born on October 19th' is repeated across several segments where the original audio almost certainly contained different content).

errors: Birth year is not stated in the transcript; only the date October 19 is recoverable. The transcript's repetition of 'October 19th' appears to be Whisper repetition artifact rather than emphasis from the speaker.
The specific city or town the family moved to after the mother's death cannot be confidently extracted (transcript reads 'moved to war with it' which is likely a Whisper error).
improvements: Re-transcribe to establish McClary's birth year, his mother's name and cause of death, and the destination of the family's move.

chapters.title: Father's Confrontation with a White Man
chapters.summary: McClary describes a story (or stories) involving his father and a confrontation with a white man, framed as a formative memory of his father not backing down from racial intimidation. The narrative is heavily fragmented by Whisper transcription errors. McClary references his father's having moved from South Carolina to Georgia at some prior point. He frames his father as someone who 'had a confrontation with a white man' and remained in town afterward — a fact he returns to at the end of the interview as the source of his own lack of fear.
related_events: Jim Crow era confrontations in Georgia

errors: The specifics of the father's confrontation (where, when, with whom, over what) are not recoverable from the Whisper transcript. Summary keeps only what McClary returns to later in the interview.
improvements: Re-transcribe to recover the narrative of the father's confrontation.

chapters.title: Schooling and Early Awareness of Local Integration Efforts
chapters.summary: McClary describes attending a Black school built specifically for one Black family in his community. He references the construction of a high school and mentions an early attempt at school integration in which an unnamed local Black family's young daughter rode a school bus and had some form of conflict with a white passenger. The transcript is heavily degraded in this section and most specifics cannot be confidently extracted.

errors: Names of the school, the family, the bus incident participants are not recoverable from the Whisper transcript.
Whether the school-integration attempt described is the family's own or a community-level event is ambiguous in the transcript.
improvements: Re-transcribe and cross-reference with school-desegregation records for the McClary family's home town.

chapters.title: Entering the Movement: Albany Movement and Charles Sherrod
chapters.summary: McClary describes how he first became involved with the movement, witnessing what he describes as a Black woman and a young white man being beaten by people he refers to as 'the campus in the old campus in the county' (Whisper degradation prevents confident reconstruction). He references that the local effort came to be called the Albany Movement, and that he wrote something for a newspaper and 'sent it in,' which is when Charles Sherrod entered his life as an organizer. McClary describes Charles Sherrod as 'an activist anyway' and recounts attending a meeting at which he formally joined what he calls 'the party' (almost certainly meaning SNCC or Sherrod's organization). He references his brother's role in organizing 'about nine counties in South[west] [Georgia]' and a plan to 'take over one of the counties' through local electoral organizing — a description consistent with Sherrod's documented Southwest Georgia Project strategy.

errors: Whether the 'nine counties' organizing effort was run by McClary's actual biological brother or by Charles Sherrod (sometimes addressed as 'brother' in movement parlance) is ambiguous in the Whisper transcript.
The 'party' McClary joined is not named in the transcript; SNCC is inferred from the Sherrod context but the actual organization name is not recoverable.
The newspaper McClary wrote for is not named.
improvements: Re-transcribe to clarify the 'brother' reference and the name of the organization joined.
Cross-reference McClary's name in published Albany Movement and Southwest Georgia Project rosters.

chapters.title: Welfare-Rights Appeals and Tenant Advocacy
chapters.summary: Asked what activities he did in the movement, McClary describes work on behalf of tenants threatened with eviction ('tenants getting the bone') and welfare-rights advocacy for people who 'were not getting welfare' or had been denied it. He describes appealing welfare denials and helping people through the welfare-appeals process. He also references that 'most of the white folks had gone' in some segregated context, and that an unnamed young woman would come collect him on a daily basis to do this work when he was at home. He references school-related advocacy as well.

errors: Names of the tenants, the young woman who collected him, the specific welfare offices, and the geographic scope of this work are not recoverable from the Whisper transcript.
Whether 'tenants getting the bone' refers to eviction, denial of repairs, or something else is inferred from limited context.
improvements: Re-transcribe to recover specifics of the welfare-appeals work, including whether McClary worked with the National Welfare Rights Organization or Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project directly.

chapters.title: Attempted Desegregation of a Local Cafe ('Casino')
chapters.summary: McClary describes an action at a local cafe in his hometown that he calls 'a casino,' where Black customers were directed to a back window rather than served at the front counter. He describes that a (likely white) activist 'came down to wake us from New York,' and recounts going down to the casino to try to be served at the front door. The detailed sequence is heavily Whisper-degraded, but the action appears to have ended with the proprietor closing the establishment. McClary indicates Charles Sherrod did not know about this particular action in advance.
related_events: Lunch counter desegregation in the South

errors: The name of the cafe (rendered 'casino' in Whisper) is almost certainly a transcription error and cannot be confidently reconstructed.
The town in which this action occurred is not clearly stated.
The name of the New York activist is not recoverable.
improvements: Re-transcribe and cross-reference desegregation actions in Southwest Georgia 1961-1965 to identify the cafe and the visiting organizer.

chapters.title: Beating at the Movement Office
chapters.summary: McClary recounts being attacked while sleeping at the movement office, located on what he calls 'Mother's Beach' (likely a Whisper error for an actual street or place name). A man broke into the office, beat him, and demanded to know the whereabouts of another organizer whom McClary did not know. The attacker held McClary on the floor and beat him before another staff member came up and intervened. The police took the attacker into custody the same night.
related_events: Violence against SNCC organizers in Southwest Georgia

errors: 'Mother's Beach' is likely a Whisper error for an actual address; the real street name cannot be recovered.
The attacker's name, the staff member who intervened, and the other organizer being sought are not recoverable from the transcript.
The date of the attack is not stated.
improvements: Re-transcribe and cross-reference SW Georgia movement office locations 1962-1965 to identify the office and the attack.

chapters.title: Long-Term Outcomes and First Black Mayor
chapters.summary: McClary reflects on the long-term effects of the organizing he was part of, including that the work eventually produced 'the first black male' (Whisper rendering, almost certainly 'first Black mayor') of a small town. He references the mayor having died and not serving a full term. He also describes a moment of marching '20 people' to a school board meeting in a small town, with the white onlookers visibly shocked. The town is named 'Norway' in the transcript — almost certainly a Whisper error for an actual town name (the Southwest Georgia Project worked in several small Black-majority towns).
related_events: Black electoral victories in Southwest Georgia

errors: The town's name ('Norway' in Whisper) is almost certainly an ASR error and cannot be confidently identified.
The first Black mayor's name and town are not recoverable from the transcript.
Specific details of the school board action are heavily degraded.
improvements: Re-transcribe and cross-reference the first Black mayors elected in Southwest Georgia towns where Sherrod's project organized.

chapters.title: Reflection on Fear and Closing
chapters.summary: Asked at the end of the interview whether he was ever afraid, McClary says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid. He returns to his father as the model for this disposition, recalling that his father had a physical confrontation with a white man and 'lived in the town' afterward, which McClary contrasts with the typical fate of Black men in such situations. The interview closes with McClary thanking Dr. Jeffries and the videographer, and the standard Library of Congress / Smithsonian closing credit.
related_events: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

errors: The interview's closing reflection is one of the more coherent sections of the transcript, but specifics about the father's confrontation remain unrecoverable from the earlier (heavily degraded) sections that introduced him.
improvements: Re-transcribe to recover the full closing reflection.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist (Southwest Georgia Project / Albany Movement)
interview_metadata.interview_location: Albany, Georgia (campus of Albany State University)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Ohio State University and the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Opening framing is standard LoC format (Segment 1-9, 00:00:00,000)

Interviewer attempts thematic structure (childhood -> entry into movement -> activities -> outcomes -> reflection) but transcript jumps abruptly due to Whisper degradation (Segment 192-200, 00:13:08,000 'apologize for the 15 or so minute delay that we had to change some stuff out' — even the on-tape interview has a mid-interview technical break that re-orients the narrative)
Closing reflection on fear (Segment 407-431, 00:26:29,600-00:27:57,600) returns to the father-confrontation theme introduced earlier, providing minimal narrative resolution
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interviewer attempts a clear thematic arc, but the transcript itself is so fragmented by Whisper transcription errors and by a mid-interview technical break that any narrative structure that may exist in the underlying audio cannot be evaluated. The recoverable text reads as a series of disconnected fragments rather than a coherent oral history. Score reflects the structure of the artifact under review, not necessarily the underlying audio.

most_vivid_moment.quote: The thing I knew I was on the floor. On the other side of the building. They were checking me.

evidence: Phoebe Putney Hospital named as birthplace (Segment 12, 00:01:12,000)
Charles Sherrod named multiple times (Segment 159, 00:11:24,000 and Segment 177, 00:12:24,000)
Albany State University and Albany, Georgia (rendered 'Albany Movement' by Whisper) named as interview location (Segment 6, 00:00:31,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Recoverable specifics from the degraded transcript are sparse: Phoebe Putney Hospital, Charles Sherrod, Albany Movement, Albany State University, and a partial date of birth (October 19). Many proper nouns appear to have been mangled by Whisper (e.g., 'Mother's Beach' for an address, 'Norway' for a town name, 'casino' for a cafe). The narrator clearly provided concrete details in the underlying audio that did not survive transcription.

most_authentic_moment.quote: I was more hopeful than I was. I was hopeful and excited than I was afraid.

vulnerability_elements: Acknowledgment of being attacked at the office
Discomfort with the interview format itself: 'I'm a little bit, you know, I be comfortable, but I don't have any notes in me' (Segment 434, 00:28:02,600)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Even through Whisper degradation, McClary's closing reflection on hope-versus-fear and his framing of his father as a model of resistance carry through with emotional weight. He also expresses discomfort with the formal interview format itself at the close, which suggests authenticity. Score is moderate rather than high because the degradation prevents fuller assessment.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: The narrative-quality dimension is severely compromised by transcript degradation. The closing reflection on hope and the father-as-model framing carry emotional authenticity through the noise, but coherent structure and vivid detail cannot be reliably evaluated from this artifact. The underlying audio almost certainly contains stronger narrative material than the SRT supports.

major_events: Albany Movement organizing (Segment 142, 00:10:43,000 'They had a new people called the Albany Movement')
Charles Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project (Segment 170-177, 00:11:53,000-00:12:24,000 'tried to organize about nine counties in South[west Georgia]')
Welfare-rights and tenant organizing in SW Georgia (Segments 202-244, 00:13:43,600-00:15:12,000)
First Black mayor in a SW Georgia town (Segments 401-405, 00:25:51,600-00:26:21,600)

key_figures.name: Charles Sherrod
key_figures.relationship: Direct supervisor / organizer who recruited McClary

organizational_involvement.organization: Albany Movement / SNCC Southwest Georgia Project (inferred)
organizational_involvement.role: Field organizer doing welfare-appeals and tenant work

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: McClary worked directly under Charles Sherrod during the canonical SW Georgia Project years and was on the receiving end of organizer-targeted violence (the office beating). If recoverable, this is a valuable insider perspective on the daily texture of Sherrod's organizing — particularly the welfare-appeals dimension, which is less documented than the better-known Albany city campaigns.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: McClary describes direct participation in Sherrod's SW Georgia organizing, the welfare-rights work, an attempted desegregation action, and a beating at the movement office. This is solid participant perspective from the canonical SW Georgia project. Score is moderate rather than high because the transcript degradation prevents confident evaluation of how close he was to specific named events.

precision_examples: Date of birth: October 19 (year not recoverable), Phoebe Putney Hospital (Segment 12, 00:01:12,000)
verifiable_claims: Charles Sherrod organized in Southwest Georgia in the 1960s (verifiable, matches civil_rights_facts.json)
The Albany Movement was a 1961-1962 desegregation campaign (verifiable, matches civil_rights_facts.json)
specificity_of_details.justification: Recoverable specifics are limited to Charles Sherrod, the Albany Movement, Phoebe Putney Hospital, Albany State University, and an October 19 birth date. The transcript is too degraded to evaluate the underlying interview's specificity; a re-transcription is required.

new_information: Welfare-rights advocacy as part of the SW Georgia Project's daily work (less-documented dimension of Sherrod's organizing)
First-person account of organizer-targeted violence at the movement office
Local cafe desegregation action that Sherrod was not aware of in advance
understudied_aspects: Welfare-rights work within the SW Georgia Project
Rank-and-file organizer experiences of the Project's daily texture
Long-term electoral outcomes ('first Black mayor' in an unnamed SW Georgia town)
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: If recoverable, McClary's account suggests that some local direct-action initiatives in SW Georgia were taken by rank-and-file organizers without Sherrod's prior knowledge, complicating any narrative that frames Sherrod as the sole strategist of all local actions.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the SW Georgia Project and the welfare-rights movement could use McClary's account to document the everyday-organizing dimension of Sherrod's work, provided the transcript is re-done. As it stands, the artifact is not citable.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: The interview promises valuable additions to the SW Georgia Project record, particularly on welfare-rights work and rank-and-file initiative. However, the transcript degradation prevents the artifact from being usable in its current form.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Historical value is moderate-to-promising based on the topics McClary attempts to cover — Sherrod, welfare-rights work, movement-office violence, long-term electoral outcomes. However, the artifact under review cannot be reliably mined for that value because of pervasive Whisper transcription errors. Re-transcription would likely raise this score substantially.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: McClary describes going from a young person witnessing local racial violence (a beating he observed near the campus) to becoming an active organizer working under Charles Sherrod. He frames his father's prior refusal-to-back-down posture as the model that shaped his own willingness to do this work. However, the transcript degradation prevents fuller reconstruction of the transformation arc.
key_turning_points: Witnessing a beating near the campus (Segment 113-127, 00:09:28,000-00:10:03,000)
Writing to the newspaper and being contacted by Charles Sherrod (Segments 156-184, 00:11:22,000-00:12:42,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: McClary moves from observer to direct organizer to violence survivor.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: The closing 'I was more hopeful than I was afraid' has some inspirational quality, but the transcript degradation prevents the full arc from registering with the listener.
transformation_growth.justification: An implicit transformation arc exists in the recoverable text but cannot be fully evaluated. Score is low because the artifact does not support stronger inferences.

peak_moments.description: The office attack: McClary woken by intruder, beaten, interrogated about another organizer's whereabouts
peak_moments.stakes: McClary's physical safety; potentially his life

peak_moments.description: Confrontation/desegregation attempt at the 'casino' cafe ending with the proprietor closing the establishment
peak_moments.stakes: Public-accommodations integration; risk of arrest or violence

peak_moments.description: Marching 20 people into a school board meeting in a small SW Georgia town
peak_moments.stakes: Local political power; demonstration of community force

tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From hope and excitement (closing reflection) to terror (office attack)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Despite the degradation, the transcript clearly conveys several high-stakes moments. The office attack and the desegregation action would, if more fully recoverable, register as dramatic peaks.

universal_themes: Hope and excitement in the face of danger
Loss of a parent in childhood
relatable_struggles: Helping neighbors navigate bureaucratic systems (welfare appeals)
Facing intimidation and choosing not to back down
human_moments: Father's example as the source of courage (Segments 411-416, 00:26:45,600-00:27:23,600)
Discomfort with being interviewed: 'I'm a little bit, you know, I be comfortable, but I don't have any notes in me' (Segment 434, 00:28:04,600)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: The intergenerational moral-model theme — father's example shaping son's courage — has wide relatability. However, the transcript degradation suppresses most of the human texture.
relatable_experiences.justification: Some universal themes are recoverable but most relatability is lost in the Whisper degradation.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Emotional resonance is suppressed by the transcript artifact, not by the underlying interview. The intergenerational courage theme, the office attack, and the closing fear-versus-hope reflection are the strongest recoverable elements. Re-transcription would likely substantially raise this score.

barriers: Severe Whisper transcription errors (estimated 60-70 percent of segments incoherent or garbled)
Speaker's actual dialect almost certainly does not impair comprehension in audio, but transcript readability is severely compromised
Whisper repetition artifacts (e.g., 'I was born on October 19th' repeated across multiple segments; 'A...' placeholder repeated across segments 244-271)
language_clarity.justification: The transcript-as-artifact is largely unreadable, regardless of how clear the underlying audio is. The score reflects the artifact under review.

high_energy_sections: Office attack narrative (Segments 309-353, 00:18:51,600-00:21:21,600) — even in degraded form conveys urgency
slow_sections: Segments 244-271 are entirely 'A...' placeholder rendered by Whisper, representing a complete loss of content for roughly 30 seconds
Segments 75-100 contain heavy 'The vehicles. The vehicles.' repetition artifacts
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Very limited for the transcript-as-text. Listeners of the actual audio may have a different experience.
narrative_flow.justification: The Whisper artifacts prevent any coherent flow assessment. The audio almost certainly is more navigable than the SRT.

themes_connected: Welfare-rights work as a movement priority (briefly)
Local-government takeover as an electoral strategy (briefly)
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: If recoverable, McClary's account of welfare-rights organizing and local electoral takeover would connect to contemporary economic-justice and voting-rights work. The current transcript does not support strong claims here.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: McClary does some implicit meaning-making in the closing reflection but the transcript degradation prevents most of it from registering.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Theme-connection is heavily suppressed by the degraded artifact.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessibility scores are uniformly low because of the transcript-artifact problems, not because of the underlying narrator. Re-transcription is the appropriate intervention before any further evaluation.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This transcript artifact does not support publication-grade evaluation of Robert McClary's testimony. The underlying interview appears to cover important topics — Charles Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project, welfare-rights organizing, a movement-office attack, and the eventual election of the 'first Black mayor' of a small SW Georgia town — but pervasive Whisper transcription errors (estimated 60-70 percent of segments incoherent or garbled, with repetition artifacts and proper-noun corruption) prevent reliable extraction of his account. The recoverable content is sufficient to confirm McClary's participation in Sherrod's organizing and to identify the general scope of his work, but not sufficient for any direct quotation, named-event attribution, or detailed narrative reconstruction. The artifact should be routed to human review and re-transcribed from source audio before any further pipeline processing.
key_strengths: Direct participation in Charles Sherrod's SW Georgia Project organizing, which is well-corroborated by the recoverable mentions of Sherrod and the 'nine counties' organizing strategy.
Coverage of welfare-rights organizing, which is an understudied dimension of the SW Georgia Movement record.
First-person account of organizer-targeted violence at the movement office, even if details are not recoverable.
key_limitations: Severe Whisper transcription degradation (estimated 60-70 percent of segments incoherent) prevents almost all detailed extraction.

Most proper nouns beyond Charles Sherrod and the Albany Movement are corrupted (e.g., 'Mother's Beach' for an address, 'Norway' for a town, 'casino' for a cafe, 'Albany Movement' substituted for 'Albany' as the city name).

claims.claim: Robert McClary was interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries on March 9, 2013 in Albany, Georgia for the Civil Rights History Project.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 3-9 (00:00:17,000-00:01:02,000): 'Today is Saturday, March 9, 2013. My name is Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries of the Ohio State University and the Southern oral history program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. I'm with videographer, Petna Dalico in Albany Movement, Georgia on the campus of Albany Movement State University to conduct an interview for the Civil Rights History Project [...] We are here this afternoon with Mr. Robert McClary.'
claims.rationale: The opening segments establish all elements of the claim. 'Albany Movement, Georgia' is a Whisper substitution for 'Albany, Georgia'; the canonical city name is used in the summary.

claims.claim: The Civil Rights History Project is a joint undertaking of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 7 (00:00:38,000): 'to conduct an interview for the Civil Rights History Project, which is a joint undertaking of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress.'

claims.claim: McClary was born at Phoebe Putney Hospital on October 19.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 12 (00:01:12,000): 'I was born on October 19th, but I'm a Phoebe Putney Hospital.'
claims.rationale: Date and place of birth are directly stated, though the year is not recoverable from the transcript.

claims.claim: McClary's mother died when he was very young.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 15 (00:01:38,000): 'My mother died on that building before I became a teacher.' Segment 22 (00:03:23,000): 'My mother died on October 19th, and she stayed there with my father.'
claims.rationale: The transcript references his mother's death, but the surrounding context is heavily degraded. The 'October 19th' rendering in Segment 22 appears to be a Whisper repetition artifact rather than an actual statement that the mother died on the same date as McClary's birth. The summary describes the death as occurring when he was young, which is supported only loosely by 'before I became a teacher.'

claims.claim: McClary's father held a job on the river and moved the surviving family after the mother's death.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 16 (00:01:48,000): 'My father had a job on the river, and he took the family that was left in the house, and we moved to war with it.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. The destination 'war with it' is a Whisper error for an actual place name and is not asserted in the summary.

claims.claim: McClary's father had a confrontation with a white man.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 59 (00:06:43,000): 'I love it, I'm fine because he has a confrontation with a white man.' Segment 415 (00:27:12,600): 'I don't have physical conversation with a black guy and live the town for black.'
claims.rationale: The confrontation is referenced twice. Segment 415's 'physical conversation' appears to be a Whisper rendering of 'physical confrontation' and the segment context (the closing reflection on the father as model) supports the summary's framing.

claims.claim: McClary's father came from South Carolina to Georgia at some point.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 55-56 (00:06:29,000-00:06:33,000): 'Before I went over from Carolina to Gigi. From South Carolina, from South Carolina.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated, though 'Gigi' is a Whisper error for Georgia.

claims.claim: McClary became involved with the movement after witnessing a beating near the campus.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 113-141 (00:09:28,000-00:10:43,000): 'When the boarding right at the side. [...] And I thought these two people coming down to me. [...] And the young white boy. And they were talking. [...] And they had a club board. [...] And they work here on people in the county. People in the street. The campus in the old campus in the county. [...] I thought they took down my dress. And they took down the hair. [...] An awful lot of changes. It was a young white guy. A black girl in the old campus in the county. [...] They had a new people called the Albany Movement.'
claims.rationale: The transcript indicates McClary witnessed something near 'the campus' that motivated his entry into what was then called the Albany Movement, but the specifics of what he witnessed are heavily degraded. The summary's framing is intentionally hedged.

claims.claim: The local organizing was identified as the Albany Movement.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 142 (00:10:43,000): 'They had a new people called the Albany Movement.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Albany Movement is a verified civil-rights organization per civil_rights_facts.json.

claims.claim: Charles Sherrod recruited McClary into the movement after McClary wrote to a newspaper.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 156-159 (00:11:20,000-00:11:25,000): 'So you wrote for the paper. Sent it in. Yeah. And then (Charles) Sherrod was like,' Segments 177-184 (00:12:19,000-00:12:42,000): '(Charles) Sherrod was an activist anyway. But I went to a meeting in... [...] And that's when I joined the party.'
claims.rationale: Charles Sherrod is named directly. The recruitment-via-newspaper sequence is supported by the transcript but the surrounding details (which newspaper, what McClary wrote, the meeting location) are not recoverable. Summary keeps only what the transcript supports.

claims.claim: Sherrod's organizing effort targeted about nine counties in Southwest Georgia, with a goal of taking over one of the counties through local electoral organizing.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 170-176 (00:11:53,000-00:12:17,000): 'My brother tried to organize about nine counties in South America. It was a pity being there. And we were going to take over one of the counties. elect locally. [...] That was planned.' Segment 374 (00:23:36,600): 'Oh, for Southwest Georgia. It's for the project.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. 'South America' is a Whisper error for 'South Georgia' or 'Southwest Georgia' (confirmed by Segment 374). The 'nine counties' organizing strategy and Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project are confirmed by civil_rights_facts.json. The 'brother' reference is ambiguous and may refer to Sherrod or to a literal brother; summary acknowledges this ambiguity in the chapter notes.

claims.claim: McClary did welfare-appeals work and tenant advocacy as part of his movement activity.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 202-241 (00:13:43,600-00:15:06,000): 'Tenants... And people are getting the bone. Tenants is getting the bone. We get the welfare activities. People... They're not getting welfare. They were giving me any welfare. [...] Every time they took them down, we used... appeal that... [...] When used to... Like becoming... Appearing through you. And that would anytime for now.'
claims.rationale: The welfare-appeals and tenant-advocacy work is supported by the transcript even through the Whisper degradation.

claims.claim: McClary attempted to desegregate a local cafe (referred to in the transcript as a 'casino') where Black customers were routed to a back window.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 286-289 (00:13:13,600-00:18:34,600): 'There was a cafe down in the hometown. I want to go see them. What was it called? A casino. A casino. And you can go into your back, go to the back window.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. The cafe name 'casino' is almost certainly a Whisper error and is preserved in quotation marks in the summary.

claims.claim: Charles Sherrod did not know about McClary's planned desegregation action in advance.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 307 (00:18:34,600): 'Can you say (Charles) Sherrod didn't know about that?'
claims.rationale: The interviewer's question confirms this framing and McClary's subsequent response continues in that vein.

claims.claim: McClary was attacked while sleeping at the movement office; a man broke in, beat him, interrogated him about another organizer's whereabouts, and was taken into custody by police the same night after another staff member intervened.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 309-353 (00:18:51,600-00:21:21,600): 'Boy, that little thing I was having a time I got attacked. I was sleeping in office. [...] And other day I had not gotten by him the door. [...] They called me with a man. I don't know me. For the guy. [...] And the payment break. The thing I knew I was on the floor. On the other side of the building. They were checking me. [...] I finally convinced him that I didn't know where this guy was. [...] One of the other staff members came up. [...] And he took over. They took away. The police got him the same night.'
claims.rationale: The attack narrative is consistently supported across the transcript segments, even with the Whisper degradation. The phrase 'I was sleeping in office' is supported by Segment 310.

claims.claim: The work eventually produced the first Black mayor of a small Southwest Georgia town who died before completing his term.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 401-405 (00:25:51,600-00:26:21,600): 'But in that little town like that, we got the first black male of one with, which was, well, it was the left during the last three because he died. But we did get a black male and one with, I wasn't there, but it's all gone with the results too then. Yes, yes.'
claims.rationale: The transcript supports the first-Black-mayor outcome and that he died. The town name is not recoverable. 'Black male' is a Whisper rendering of 'Black mayor.'

claims.claim: Asked whether he was ever afraid, McClary said he was more hopeful and excited than afraid, and framed his father as the model for his courage.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 407-416 (00:26:29,600-00:27:23,600): 'Were you ever afraid? [...] I was more hopeful than I was. I was more hopeful than I was. I was hopeful and excited than I was afraid. I remember when I say my father, he always complained about other people being told to be intimate. [...] And he would not take it out of the way, get it, revive, but like I tell you, he's the only man who's been told to be a black male. I don't have physical conversation with a black guy and live the town for black.'
claims.rationale: Directly supported. 'Black male' in this context is again a Whisper rendering of 'Black mayor' or possibly a misrendering of 'Black man' (the elder McClary is being held up as the only Black man who confronted a white man and lived in the town afterward, given the era).

publication_decision.rationale: This transcript artifact does not meet the publication threshold and is routed to human review. The main summary's accuracy score (60) and quality score (35) both fall well below the 90/90 publication threshold, and four citation-audit claims are partially_supported because the underlying transcript is too Whisper-degraded to confidently establish them. The artifact-level problem (estimated 60-70 percent of segments incoherent or garbled, with extensive ASR repetition artifacts and proper-noun corruption) is the dominant blocker rather than the summarizer's work. The appropriate disposition is re-transcription from source audio before any further pipeline processing. This routing is consistent with the OPEN_PROBLEMS Problem 1a flag for this transcript. Charles Sherrod, the Albany Movement, the SW Georgia Project, and the welfare-rights/tenant/office-attack/first-Black-mayor narrative threads are all confidently identifiable from the recoverable text and should guide the re-transcription priorities.
